summary: Uncle Bob defends his association of TDD with professionalism while acknowledging respected programmers who disagree, including Jim Coplien, Rich Hickey, and DHH. He draws a parallel to Ignaz Semmelweis, who faced decades of resistance when advocating hand-washing in medicine. Martin argues that TDD is not currently a prerequisite for professionalism but believes it will become one as software failures increasingly endanger society. He acknowledges he could be wrong, but points to mounting casualties from software failures as evidence that something must change. The piece balances conviction with humility, refusing to call current TDD skeptics unprofessional while predicting TDD may eventually carry the force of law.
key_points: TDD is associated with professionalism but not currently a prerequisite for it—that may change in the future
The resistance to TDD mirrors the 60-year resistance doctors had to Semmelweis's hand-washing protocols
Respected programmers like Coplien, Hickey, and DHH who reject TDD are still professionals—being wrong about a practice doesn't make one unprofessional in that moment of history
Software failures are mounting (Healthcare.gov, Knight Capital, Toyota) and society's dependence on software makes this an existential concern
If TDD proves as significant as hand-washing was to medicine, it may eventually carry legal weight
money_quotes: I plead guilty to claiming that the association exists.
Their only fault was that they were human.
But patients are dying out there!
Something must change… or we are headed for a catastrophe of existential magnitude.
It would not surprise me if, one day, TDD had the force of law behind it.
I won't call you unprofessional, and I won't think you are unprofessional. Because today, at this moment in our history, TDD is not the prerequisite of professionalism that I believe it will become.
If that is the case, so be it.
key_insight: Professional standards evolve with knowledge—what seems optional today may become mandatory tomorrow, and the measure of professionalism is doing the best you can with what you know at the time.
